sources.llm_title: Master of Information Management and Systems | UC Berkeley School of Information
sources.llm_summary: Program overview describing MIMS as a two-year, STEM-designated, full-time professional master's program with interdisciplinary curriculum, career outcomes, and project-based learning.

sources.llm_title: MIMS Degree Requirements | UC Berkeley School of Information
sources.llm_summary: Degree requirements page stating the program is a two-year full-time master's requiring at least 48 semester units, with core courses, electives, and a final project.

sources.llm_title: MIMS Admissions | UC Berkeley School of Information
sources.llm_summary: Admissions page identifying the program as MIMS and situating it within the UC Berkeley School of Information.

sources.llm_title: Funding Your MIMS Education | UC Berkeley School of Information
sources.llm_summary: Funding page with tuition, fees, estimated expenses, and student support information for MIMS.

llm_rationales.note: Selected interdisciplinary because the page says MIMS offers a 'uniquely interdisciplinary curriculum that bridges technology, people, and policy.'
Selected professional because the page describes MIMS as a 'two-year STEM-designated, full-time professional master’s program' and highlights career outcomes and internships.
Selected stem_adjacent because the program is framed around technology leadership, AI, data science, machine learning, and software-related core knowledge areas.
Selected social_impact because the page explicitly references ethical technology, public good, policy, and graduates shaping products that improve lives.
The program page says MIMS is a 'two-year' full-time professional master's program and notes the final project culminates in the 'two year program,' which corresponds to four Berkeley semesters.
The handbook states students are required to enroll in 12 units each semester and 'complete the program within the two-year time frame' / 'complete the degree in 4 semesters.'

Used the MIMS funding page's 'Total Tuition & Fees' annual figure of $33,880.50 for California residents and divided by the program's two-year / four-semester structure to estimate tuition + mandatory fees only for cross-program comparison. This excludes living expenses and other COA items.

Used the same MIMS funding page's 'Total Tuition & Fees' annual figure of $33,880.50 for California residents as the program-stated tuition-and-fees total scope; evidence supports a single annual tuition-and-fees figure, not a full bundled COA total.
The materials give a first-year core and a semester-by-semester suggested sequence, but not every required element is term-mapped; the second-year electives are broad and flexible.
The source names five required core courses and a final project course, but the schema only allows integer or null sequence_position values, so term labels were omitted.
The degree plan places the core in the first year and the final project in the fourth semester, but not every required item has a specific term mapping in the extracted evidence.

identity.program_name: Master of Information Management and Systems
identity.credential_name: Master of Information Management and Systems

positioning.positioning_summary: MIMS is positioned as a two-year professional master’s program for aspiring technology leaders that blends technology, people, and policy. The program emphasizes interdisciplinary study, hands-on project-based learning, and career preparation across UX, AI/data, product, and information policy, with an explicit public-interest and ethical technology orientation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MIMS is a two-year, full-time program requiring at least 48 semester units. Students complete five required core courses in the first year, then take additional advanced technology and social science/policy coursework plus electives in the second year, with the degree culminating in a final project. The curriculum is customizable and students are expected to specialize through electives and advanced study.

core_courses.course_summary: Introduces conceptual modeling, semantic representation, vocabulary and metadata design, classification, standardization, and information retrieval practices.
learning_outcomes: Explain conceptual modeling and semantic representation
Design vocabularies, metadata, and classification schemes
Apply information retrieval practices and technologies
Analyze textual and non-textual information formats
Use computational processes for organizing information

core_courses.course_summary: Required core course introducing information and information technology in society, including social impacts and social science methods.
learning_outcomes: Explain how information technologies affect social groups and social interaction.
Analyze information issues at individual, group, organizational, and societal levels.
Identify social problems associated with information and information technology.
Apply qualitative and quantitative social science methods to information questions.
Evaluate theories of technology in society and networked sociability.

core_courses.course_summary: Required core course examining legal and ethical issues in information systems, including privacy, copyright, consumer protection, security, and policy frameworks.
learning_outcomes: Analyze legal and ethical issues in information technologies.
Apply privacy, copyright, consumer protection, and security frameworks.
Evaluate how law shapes system architecture and data practices.
Reflect on the role of information professionals in legal work.

core_courses.course_summary: Required core course introducing the basics of computer programming for computer science, data science, and information management.
learning_outcomes: Write interactive Python programs
Analyze data and process text
Draw graphics and manipulate images
Simulate physical systems
Apply problem decomposition and efficient programming style

core_courses.course_title: Introduction to Data Structures and Analytics
core_courses.course_summary: Required core course introducing data structures and data analysis in Python, with emphasis on coding best practices.
learning_outcomes: Represent, manipulate, and analyze structured data sets
Apply fundamentals of data structures and data analysis in Python
Write code using best practices throughout the course
Use the course as the second half of the INFO 206A sequence

core_courses.course_title: Directed Group Work on Final Project
core_courses.course_summary: The final project integrates skills and concepts from the MIMS program through sustained, coherent work producing a tangible deliverable.
learning_outcomes: Integrate skills and concepts learned in the MIMS program
Carry out a sustained, coherent course of work
Produce a tangible work product
Practice project management
Present work in written form
Present work in oral form
Work in a multidisciplinary team when appropriate

curriculum.elective_requirements: Students must take at least one advanced technology course and one advanced social science or policy course in addition to the required core courses. MIMS students must take a minimum of 40 units from the Info course catalog, which may include up to 6 units of individual study or group study. Up to 8 units taken in other UC Berkeley departments may count toward the degree.

elective_courses.course_id: Advanced social science or policy course
